did_you_encounter_any_barriers:
The main barriers I encountered were around time, information overload at the start, and a couple of practical constraints at home and at work.

1. Time and routine:
I work full-time as a secondary school teacher and have two young children (ages 6 and 9), so fitting some of the suggested activities into our weekday routine was difficult. For example, on days when I had after-school meetings or parents’ evenings, I struggled to complete the longer reflection tasks or the recommended 30–40 minutes of research on environmental topics. I often ended up doing these quite late at night, which made it harder to concentrate properly.

2. Access to local options:
Some of the suggested behaviours, like using more active travel or public transport, were harder for me because of where I live. I’m in a small village outside Cardiff with limited bus services in the evenings and at weekends. I had good intentions to leave the car at home more often, but the last bus back from the city centre is quite early, which isn’t always practical with the children’s clubs and my work commitments.

3. Cost and availability of sustainable products:
I wanted to switch more quickly to low-waste or plastic-free products (for example, refillable cleaning products and shampoo bars), but some of the options available locally are more expensive than the supermarket versions. Also, the nearest refill shop is in central Cardiff, which I don’t visit every week. As a result, I had to make changes more gradually than I initially hoped.

4. Conflicting information and greenwashing:
When I tried to research things like the most sustainable types of packaging or the true impact of different diets, I came across a lot of conflicting advice. Some brands market themselves as “eco-friendly” but don’t provide clear evidence, and I found it hard to know which claims to trust. This slowed me down because I didn’t want to make changes that were just tokenistic.

5. Family habits and buy‑in:
My husband and kids were generally supportive, but there was some resistance to certain changes, like reducing meat in our meals and turning the heating down a degree or two. We had a few minor arguments about comfort vs. carbon footprint, especially on colder evenings. Getting everyone on board consistently is still a work in progress.

6. Digital barriers:
Most of the challenge materials were fine to access, but a couple of the recommended videos and external resources were blocked on my school laptop due to IT restrictions. I had to remember to revisit them at home on my personal device, and I’ll admit I forgot once or twice.

Overall, the barriers didn’t stop me participating, but they did mean I had to adapt the challenge to fit my circumstances and accept that I couldn’t do everything perfectly every day.
if_likely_please_state_which:
I am very likely to adopt several of the behaviours on a long‑term basis, and in some cases I’ve already started building them into our family routine:

1. Cutting down on meat and dairy:
We’ve already moved to having at least three vegetarian dinners a week (often more), and I plan to keep that up. For example, we now have a regular lentil bolognese night instead of minced beef, and we’ve swapped chicken fajitas for black bean and vegetable fajitas most weeks. I’ve also started using oat milk in my morning coffee at home instead of cow’s milk.

2. Reducing food waste:
I’ve begun planning our weekly meals more carefully and checking what’s already in the fridge and cupboards before doing the big shop. I’m using the freezer more intentionally, for example freezing leftover bread, cooked rice, and portions of soup. I’ve also started a “use‑up” shelf in the fridge where I put things that need to be eaten soon so they don’t get forgotten.

3. Being more energy conscious at home:
We’ve turned our thermostat down from 21°C to 19.5–20°C and are trying to put on an extra layer before turning the heating up. I’m also being more disciplined about turning off lights when leaving a room, not leaving devices on standby, and only running the washing machine and dishwasher when they’re full. I’ve set reminders to check our smart meter occasionally so I can actually see the impact.

4. Choosing lower‑impact transport when realistic:
I can’t completely give up the car because of my commute and the children’s schedules, but I am walking or cycling for shorter local trips, like going to the local shop or taking the kids to the park. I’ve also started car‑sharing with a colleague one or two days a week when our timetables line up, which reduces both costs and emissions.

5. Buying less and buying better:
I’m making a more conscious effort to avoid impulse purchases, especially clothes and home decor. I’ve started using Vinted and local charity shops to buy second‑hand items for the children (like school jumpers and books), and when I do buy new, I’m checking whether items are durable and repairable. I’m also trying to avoid fast fashion brands where possible.

6. Reducing single‑use plastic:
We’ve switched to reusable water bottles for everyone in the family, and I keep tote bags in the car and by the front door so I don’t forget them when shopping. I’ve started buying larger refill packs of some cleaning products to cut down on packaging, and I’m experimenting with bar soap and shampoo bars (still finding which ones work best for my hair).

7. Talking about environmental issues more openly:
As a teacher, I’ve started weaving more environmental examples into my lessons where it’s relevant to the curriculum. At home, we’ve introduced a short “planet check‑in” at Sunday dinner, where we talk about one thing we did that week that was good for the environment and one thing we could improve. It sounds a bit cheesy, but it’s actually helping the kids understand why we’re making changes.

8. Supporting local and seasonal food:
I’ve started paying more attention to where our food comes from and am trying to buy more Welsh or UK‑grown produce when it’s in season. For instance, I’ve swapped imported strawberries in winter for apples and pears that are grown closer to home, and I’m using the local greengrocer more often instead of just relying on the big supermarket.

I don’t expect to be perfect, but these feel like realistic long‑term changes that fit my lifestyle, and the challenge has helped me see that even small, consistent changes can add up over time.
please_state_which:
The challenge has definitely deepened my awareness of several key environmental issues, even though I considered myself fairly environmentally conscious beforehand. Some of the areas where my understanding has grown include:

1. Climate change and everyday energy use:
I knew about climate change in general terms, but the challenge helped me connect it much more directly to everyday decisions, like how we heat our home, what kind of transport we use, and how often we buy new electronic devices. Seeing the breakdown of household emissions (heating vs. transport vs. food, etc.) was eye‑opening and made it clear that small choices, like turning the thermostat down slightly or sharing car journeys, actually do make a measurable difference.

2. Food systems, diet, and land use:
I was aware that meat has a higher environmental impact, but I hadn’t fully appreciated just how significant this is, especially for beef and lamb. The materials explaining land use, deforestation, and methane emissions really stuck with me. It also made me think more critically about where our food comes from, the role of intensive agriculture, and the importance of reducing food waste as one of the easiest “wins” for the climate.

3. Plastic pollution and microplastics:
I already tried to avoid obvious single‑use plastics like carrier bags and straws, but I hadn’t realised how widespread microplastics are, from synthetic clothing fibres to the breakdown of tyres and packaging. Learning that microplastics are now being found in rivers, oceans, and even in human blood and lungs was honestly quite shocking. It made me reassess things like buying cheap synthetic clothes and how often I replace household items.

4. Biodiversity loss and habitat fragmentation:
Before the challenge, I tended to think mainly about carbon emissions, but the activities around local nature and biodiversity really broadened my view. I learned more about how important hedgerows, small woodlands, and even pollinator‑friendly gardens are for supporting wildlife in Wales. It made me see our own small garden as part of a bigger network of habitats, not just a private space.

5. Links between environmental issues and social justice:
One of the most impactful parts of the challenge was learning how environmental problems don’t affect everyone equally. The materials on climate justice and the idea that vulnerable communities—both globally and within Wales—are often hit hardest by pollution, flooding, and rising energy costs helped me connect environmental issues with fairness, health, and poverty. It also helped me understand the thinking behind Wales’ Well‑being of Future Generations Act.

6. The specific context of Wales’ environmental goals:
I had heard of the Well‑being of Future Generations Act but didn’t really know what it meant in practice. Through the challenge, I learned more about Wales’ specific targets around emissions reduction, renewable energy, and protecting nature, and how these are linked to the seven well‑being goals. It made me feel more connected to what’s happening at a national level rather than seeing environmental issues as something distant or purely global.

7. The importance of community‑level action:
I also became more aware of how much impact local groups and community projects can have—things like community gardens, repair cafés, and local litter‑picks. It made me realise that being environmentally responsible isn’t only about individual lifestyle choices; it’s also about getting involved in community initiatives and supporting local decision‑making that prioritises sustainability.

Overall, the challenge has taken my awareness from a fairly generalised concern about climate change and “being eco‑friendly” to a much more detailed understanding of specific issues and how they’re interconnected.
